sections.text: Welcome to English One, Module One — Reading Comprehension Strategies. Reading is not just decoding the words on the page. Today you learn four moves that turn passive reading into active understanding — find the main idea, support it with details, draw inferences from evidence, and recognize how the text is built.

Here is the trap. Most students read a paragraph once, glance at the first sentence, and call that the main idea. Sometimes it works. Often it does not. The main idea is the point the writer wants to make, and you only see it after you read the whole passage. One pass is not reading.

Game plan. First, learn to spot the main idea and the details that support it. Second, learn to make inferences using text evidence, not guesses. Third, learn to recognize five common text structures. Then we pause twice so you practice the moves on short passages before the lesson ends.

Main idea is the writer's central point about the topic. Topic is what the passage is about. Main idea is what the writer says about that topic. Supporting details are the facts, examples, and reasons that prove the main idea. Ask — if I removed this sentence, would the main idea still stand?

The most common mistake — assuming the first sentence is always the main idea. Often the first sentence is the hook, a question, or a vivid example. The real main idea may sit in the middle paragraph or be stated nowhere directly. When that happens, you write it yourself, using the supporting details as evidence.

An inference is what the text strongly implies but does not state outright. It is not a wild guess. The recipe — text evidence plus your reasoning equals the inference. If you cannot point to the line that supports your inference, you are guessing, not reading. Always cite the evidence.

Writers build paragraphs five common ways. Description piles up traits. Sequence orders events in time. Compare and contrast lines up similarities and differences. Cause and effect links a trigger to an outcome. Problem and solution names a trouble and answers it. Spot the structure, and the meaning becomes much easier to track.

Your turn. Read this short passage twice. Identify the main idea in one sentence, then list three supporting details. Remember — the main idea is the writer's point, not the first sentence by default. Pause now. Solve it. Press play when you are ready.

Time. The main idea is that daily reading improves student writing in measurable ways. Three details support it. One — students who read twenty minutes a day scored higher on writing tests. Two — vocabulary grew by hundreds of words in a year. Three — daily readers used more varied sentence structures. The first sentence was a vivid hook, not the main point.

One more. Read this paragraph and name the text structure used. Look for signal words — first, next, because, however, problem, solution, similar, different. The signal words almost always give the structure away. Pause now. Choose one of the five structures. Press play when ready.

Time. The signal words here are first, then, finally, and after that. Those are sequence words — the paragraph orders steps in time. So the structure is sequence. Description would describe traits. Cause and effect would explain why. Compare and contrast would line two things up. Problem and solution would name a trouble. Sequence is the answer.

Now put the moves together — the summary. A good one-paragraph summary names the topic, states the main idea, includes the two or three strongest supporting details, and notes the structure used. Skip your opinion. Skip side details. If your summary takes longer to read than the passage itself, you are copying, not summarizing.

Recap. Four moves. Main idea plus supporting details. Inference equals text evidence plus reasoning. Five structures — description, sequence, compare and contrast, cause and effect, problem and solution. Summary equals topic plus main idea plus key details plus structure. Read the passage twice. Mark the structure. Then write.

How to actually master this. Read the Purdue OWL writing lab page on reading strategies. Run the Khan Academy practice on main idea and making inferences. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the assignment — one article and one story, with a summary, two inferences, and the structure for each. Book an advisor check-in if either pause felt shaky. Next up — Module Two, Parts of Speech.
